yeah, origin story to begin.
right.
So, I mean, really going back about three or four years ago, Ed and Elliot approached me about doing something media wise with them.
And at the time, I was pretty satisfied with what I was doing at TechCrunch with the general, you know, goings on there.
And, you know, at the time, I said, you know, I'm flattered, but, you I didn't want to pursue it much further than an initial discussion that we had.
And I kind of let it drop.
And then last year, almost exactly a year ago, you know, things were beginning to shift at TC and I wasn't totally happy.
Happy anymore.
Without getting into that politics too much, I think you know what was going on.
I was very unhappy with what happened to you and Matt, for starters, and a bunch of other people.
But there was just generally something every week that pissed me off.
And at some point, I was like, you know what, I'm just going to send out some feelers, right?
And I had a conversation with several people I consider mentors.
And, you know, one of them said to me, and she's somebody that like really got me going in tech journalism.
And like 25 years ago, I have a lot of respect for her.
And she said, you know, don't just make a lateral move.
Don't just like go to the verge and be the enterprise reporter.
You know, don't do that.
Build something.
Like have something that you, you can create from scratch that you can say, I built this.
I'm like, you know, that's
The attractive thought.
And, you know, sort of pulling back from my own personal perspective, you know, I'm a lot closer to the end of my career than I am beginning.
And I kind of have to decide what do I want to do, you know, with the time I have left in terms of, you know, career runway.
And, you know, in that context as well, like building something was kind of attractive.
So I approached several different angles.
I went to like kind of a conventional publication and I went to a brand and I talked to Ed and Elliot.
And, you know, we kind of started having this conversation in April.
I didn't get hired till August.
So it was a long, ongoing conversation.
But I started to think about what I wanted this to look like and I started to communicate that to them.
And and we, we sort of,

Between us came up with this idea that we would have this blog and this newsletter, and the blog would feature CIOs, CISOs, CTOs, people who sat technology policy in real ways inside organizations, and that they would be from significant companies.
So, you know, not startups, but, you know, significant companies.
a little bit, even though I was an enterprise reporter and I covered those big companies, you know, a lot of my emphasis was still on the startup community.
So this was a shift for me.
And I would write articles that would kind of be about the trends that were, you know, impacting those people that I was writing about.
And then there would be the newsletter.
And the newsletter's purpose was to drive traffic back to the blog, right?

I mean, I would write.
I

I mean, I kind of had the commentary in mind and the news briefs.

But, you know, the general idea was this network of executives.
And eventually, you know, because even though this is a journalistic exercise, it's still, you know, at the end of the day, kind of a content marketing piece.

relies upon the thing you want to do, which is produce quality journalism, right?

because, yeah, it has a reputation or...

You know, so it was kind of challenging, but, you know, if you're going to a PR rep and you're saying, I want to interview your chief technology officer and, you know, your, you know, Goldman Sachs or something like that, which is the kind of people that I've been talking to, you know, you don't want to go, I don't have a direct connection to the person I spoke to at Goldman Sachs.

She's a big executive, and she has people between her, and I spoke to her players, you know, and they approved me to talk to her, and I can't now suddenly go to them and say, can you introduce me, can, you know, can you let Belinda know I want her to meet this company, because it's not the...
Nature of how the relationship was from, right?

I saw you wrote about her.

wanted some significant names.

mean, within the first week, I had like five big names.
had like Goldman Sachs and, you know, this CTO, think, of Snowflake and, you know, somebody from Databricks.

the thing you produce is quality, right?

Yeah, I mean, I think there's something to be said to have that kind of knowledge.

And we did.
We knew it better than anybody.
And I think that's why it's kind of such a shame that it is kind of...

I think it is.

Like, what do you think about the trend generally?
I forget who all was, like, named in this LinkedIn post, but the one I remember because she's Chris Velasco's wife was Sarah Tibkin, right?
Like, she's going to NVIDIA to run their, like, technical blog or whatever, right?
Like, their developer story blog.
Like, so...
Like, what do you think about this trend of people high up or like experienced media professionals being brought on to do this?
Do you think the thinking is, do you think they share similar goals to kind of what Ed and Elliot wanted to do with you and with Fast Forward?
Like, is it all a piece or do you think they're kind of like different reasons depending on the places really?
I think there's probably a spectrum, you know.

So if you look at people who go to brands in general, you know, then you look at people who go to, you know, venture capital firms and the VC folks, they definitely have, like you have D&E Creighton who's doing like this, you know, he's really doing his thing.
It's kind of related to what his firm is doing, but not directly, right?
It has to be similar, it's kind of, if you really simplify it from like a VC, like traditionalist VC perspective, it's
It's like the effort to build like a customer council or whatever, like people that you can tap to advise or, yes, sometimes buy like from your portfolio or whatever.
But basically like a network of really well-placed, experienced operators like who do see sweet jobs places, right?
Like, but it's much like I think yours is a similar effort, like achieves the same end result, hopefully, eventually, but in a very different, probably more naturalistic and like lasting way.
And then Danny's, think, is similar in terms of the intended outcome, but I don't know if that's your read, right?
It's like, let's be in these people's orbits in a way that is serious and lasting and not just like transactional.
Right.
And again, yeah, like making sure that it isn't just transactional is super important to me.
And I think that's what makes it work.
And hopefully, as I said, that will kind of, you know, create a flywheel effect where you have this group of people who want.
To, you know, you know, then get together and chat and, you know, something organic and some alchemy happens.
But, you know, sort of to get back to your question, you know, there are firms who do have writers who write about, like about the portfolio companies or they do features on the partners.
I mean, it's much more of a kind of pure content marketing kind of effort.
And there's nothing wrong with that.
I mean, I think that that's a good thing to be doing.
A lot of firms need to be doing that to kind of get their word out.
And as things become more competitive, certainly in venture capital, having that kind of, you know, push into the marketplace helps on a lot of levels.
But you're right.
There are there are a number of ways that to go about this.
But a lot of journalists are going that way because.
You know, frankly, there's just fewer and fewer jobs.

I think these type of jobs, if you have, you know, the experience that we all have, you know, and certainly the example that you gave of the woman who went to NVIDIA, you know, she's been around a long time.
And, you know, they're going to pay better, probably, than most journalism jobs did.
So there's that element of it.
But, you know, it's also like you want to do this work.
You want to, you know, continue writing.
And it's becoming, you have to become more creative about how you use your skill set.
And, you know, a lot of people think you can just sort of throw something in the chat GPT and you get some words out and that's good enough.
But if you really want a quality product, you know, you have to hire people who understand.
And, you know, how to write and how to write stories and, you know, how it all fits together and, you know, kind of see the big picture and see the trends.
And, you know, you can get words that sound good on paper, but I think it really, professional writers would like to think will still have a role.
And this world in which we find ourselves, this kind of media meltdown that we find ourselves, we're going to have to have more individual kinds of, you know, small properties.
So you look at, like, what Casey Newton's doing, you know, platformer news.
You look what the folks at 404 Media are doing, you know, there's like three or four of them, really good journalists, you know.
A newcomer, he has like a small little team, but they're doing a great sort of like slightly more expansive version of what Casey's doing, right?
It's not one person.
It's more like 404, actually, but yeah, yeah.
Tom Krasa, who's an enterprise reporter who runs a publication called Runtime, which is really good.
So, you know, I think that the future is these kinds of, you know, smaller organizations where, because the cost of the overhead cost of running a media company, it just seems to be too high.
Or, you know, for companies to make a reasonable, whatever they consider a reasonable amount of money doing it.
So if you have a smaller team, then, you you lean and you can move quickly like a startup.
So to some extent, that's happening inside companies, that's happening inside venture capital firms.
And, you know, the skills that we all bring to the table.
Applicable across that broad spectrum of use cases.
So the question is then, how do you get young people to get that experience?
And that becomes a much bigger challenge because a lot of the entry-level jobs are kind of seem to be leveling away.
Right.
Yeah, that is tricky.
mean, yeah, like it's not, there's no need for like a large, not no need, but there's no economic incentive, I guess, for large cohorts.
Of like junior writers, right?
Like if you're lucky, the newcomer, let's take for example, right?
Like newcomer maybe will bring on like one intern or whatever, like per cycle, per year, whatever it is.
But he's not going to staff a newsroom with a bunch of newbie writers and then have them all train up under his guidance or whatever, right?
his newsroom's guidance.
So yeah, it's going to be like much more one-to-one bespoke, even in the apprenticeship stage.
Thank you.
Which will be a big challenge, right?
But I do wonder, like, I think there's something in that all of these brands hiring journalists, to me, is just a basic signal.
Like, yes, on the journalist side, it's like, well, I would like to continue to do what I'm doing, and I would also like to eat or, you know, enjoy my life.

But I think on the brand side, there's the, it signals to me a recognition that, like, people want to build quality, value, businesses producing actual useful, I mean, content for lack of a better word, but, you know, like, producing something useful and people actually want to engage with and read, that is not necessarily directly tied to immediate economic benefit for them, right?
Like, like, everybody seems to want to have, build and own an audience of their own.
I think this is, like, another symptom of, like, what is changing with search and with discovery and with, like, answer engines and stuff like that.
Like, Verge, I think, had a very, like, good insight in that it was, early on, they were like, okay, we need to own our audience as a destination.
Like, we need to provide them a reason that people come here, go to their address bars, type in theverge.com, and look at it, right?
And I think brands are also recognizing that.

Like, they're like, oh, the only way to, like, stand out for your customer base or even your potential customer base is to, like, give people a reason to consistently come back and engage with you directly.

You know, even if, let's say, only 5% of those people end up converting or something, it's still better than the alternative where you're invisible to search because search doesn't even work that way anymore or whatever, right?
So I think there's something of that in there.
I mean, it's the whole inbound marketing kind of ethos, right?
mean, philosophy that you're going to create, you know, you're
Nobody's a brochure anymore.
Nobody's going to read your brochure.
Nobody's going to come to your website, really.
And it's really hard to be found.
So how do you get people's attention?
And, you know, quality content is still a way to get people's attention.
And, you know, I think there's this notion now of many audiences, you know, like, you know, you don't have this mass audience.
have this audience of people who care pretty deeply about what you're doing rather than a bunch of people who kind of casually kind of flip through you, you know, a couple of times a month.
It's people.
I mean, you know, again, I don't have this huge amount of, you know, reach yet.
Hopefully I'll get there someday.
But, you know, I think what's more important is that I have a group of people who, you know, really are interested in what I, what I'm writing about.
So.
Yeah, Venture, especially at smaller firms, it's perfect for that, right?
Because it's like you really just need to narrow cast exactly the group of people that are in your founder bucket or the slight halo around them or whatever.
You don't really need to reach anybody else beyond that, right?
Right.
So, yeah, and I think that the idea now maybe is to get a group of, you know, loyal readers rather than, you know, this kind of math.
You get huge numbers and they're on the site for 12 seconds.
which was our old game.
I mean, we tried to play both games, let's say, at the same time, but yeah.
Yeah, that's great.

I mean, this is all very useful and I think, like, directionally, like, I will say it's funny because, like, Ron, you probably don't know that much about, like, the things we're building, but they are all.

Like there's a lot of AI dependency, but then the reason Matthew's bringing in me and Megan or whatever is like one of the early insights was like none of this stuff makes sense unless you have seasoned editors going through it and making opinionated calls about like what needs to change in the stuff that is produced.
Right.
And that's what we're finding.

It makes the biggest difference is like having someone who has a good is a good reader effectively or like, you know, is a good even junior editor or peer editor or whatever can come in and just be like, oh, these this part doesn't make any sense.
And this part misses the bigger picture like you're talking about.
Right.
And then feeding that big back into the model.

So it's still, you know, time saving versus what the traditional would have been, but, but it's all very specific for clients who like for who never would have dreamed of having this to begin with because it was like, who are they going to hire to write this stuff because they have hyper specific products or audiences or needs or whatever.
Right.
So it's trying to be like, oh, yeah, you can all kind of like maintain your own thing as if it was a fully staffed publication, even though there is no staff to actually generate one of these.
Right.
Like for all the various like fintech API providers or whatever it might be in the world.
Right.
Right.
But yeah, it is interesting.
I mean, sure is that where what happens, but that's a start.

I always think that if you think about kind of the original large language models, which were trained on, you know, the Internet and, you know, now they're like trying to pull in just about everything into these large, largest models.
You know, at some point you need original material because otherwise it's going to get derivative.
You know, it's just going to be like the same core group of.
Stuff that the model is kind of drawing on.
And, you know, I wonder if it can be sustained that way.
And they talk about, you know, synthetic data.
But, I mean, then you have models creating the data that they're training themselves on.
You know, I mean, it just gets kind of weirdly meta and derivative.
And you wonder how far that can go before it just sounds very generic.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, I do think about that.
Because it does tend, like, and even, like, leaving aside the larger thing of, like, what happens when all the inputs are bad.
Even when all the inputs are all just flat, like all the original creations that people put on the internet without talking about synthetically created data.
Like, the LLMs, their entire purpose is to gear towards the meme, right?

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss Ron Miller's role at Boldstart Ventures and the broader trend of journalists moving to content roles at tech companies and VC firms.](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Ron joined Boldstart to create a high-quality blog and newsletter featuring CxO interviews, aiming to build a network that could benefit portfolio companies](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [The shift of journalists to brand/VC content roles reflects both industry changes and companies' desire for quality, audience-building content](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [AI tools are impacting content creation, but human expertise remains crucial for producing truly valuable, original material](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Topics

### Fast Forward Origin and Purpose

  - [Ed and Elliot from Boldstart approached Ron 3-4 years ago about a media project](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=368.0)
  - [Ron initially declined, but reconsidered in 2022 due to changes at TechCrunch](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=408.0)
  - [Launched in December 2022 after months of planning and initial interviews](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=521.0)
  - [Focus on featuring CIOs, CISOs, CTOs from significant companies, not startups](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=563.0)
  - [Goal: Build a network of executives that could eventually benefit portfolio companies through events and connections](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=676.0)

### Content Strategy and Growth

  - [Initial challenge of attracting high-profile guests without an established publication](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=795.0)
  - [Targeted 4-5 high-quality company names to start (e.g. Salesforce CIO, Blackstone CISO)](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1000.0)
  - [Experienced "FOMO effect" with PR teams reaching out after initial interviews published](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1086.0)
  - [Now receiving inbound requests from major tech companies (Goldman Sachs, Snowflake, Databricks)](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1100.0)
  - [Newsletter drives traffic to blog; includes weekly commentary, blog highlights, news briefs](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=593.0)

### Journalism Trends and Brand Content

  - [Increasing trend of experienced journalists moving to content roles at tech companies/VCs](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1342.0)
  - [Reasons include fewer traditional journalism jobs and better compensation](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1520.0)
  - [Companies recognize value of quality content for building and owning audiences](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1784.0)
  - [Shift from mass audiences to engaged niche audiences who deeply care about specific topics](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1877.0)
  - [Challenge: How to train new journalists when entry-level positions are disappearing](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=1683.0)

### AI and Content Creation

  - [Ron experiments with AI tools like Claude for proofreading and editing assistance](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=2277.0)
  - [Mixed results: Helpful for catching repeated phrases, but also makes errors (e.g. suggesting non-existent changes)](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=2306.0)
  - [Concern about AI models becoming derivative if not trained on fresh, original content](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=2090.0)
  - [Human expertise still crucial for producing truly valuable, insightful content](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=2554.0)
  - [Discussion of Google I/O announcements and OpenAI's marketing approach](https://fathom.video/share/_caU1dA66_-sYM43ydAqoEGFQXagUPyp?tab=summary&timestamp=2205.0)

## Next Steps

  - Darrell to potentially follow up with any additional questions not covered in the conversation
  - Ron open to clarifying or expanding on any topics discussed
  - Darrell to incorporate insights into a publication about different approaches to marketing, growth, and content in tech/VC
